coredata.dc:title: Harnessing multi-target nanosweepers inhibiting β-amyloid aggregation, scavenging reactive oxygen species and overcoming the blood brain barrier for rescuing Alzheimer’s disease

coredata.dc:description: Ideal therapeutic strategies for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) should be inhibiting β-amyloid (Aβ) aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) on the premise that drugs can cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB). However, efficient AD therapy is unrealizable due to the lack of multi-target therapy function and poor BBB penetration. Herein, multi-target nanosweepers (FA-LCDs) prepared with ferulic acid (FA) and L-glutamate-derived carbon dots (LCDs) are developed to penetrate the BBB, inhibit Aβ fibrillation, degrade Aβ plaque and clear excessive ROS. Interestingly, although FA has excellent ROS clearance ability, it promotes the aggregation of amyloid, which has potential hidden dangers for treating AD. Rationally designing, modifying FA on the surface of LCDs endows FA-LCDs superior therapeutic performance compared with FA or LCDs alone, and even realizes the superposition of performance. Consequently, the FA-LCDs can target Aβ binding for effectively inhibiting Aβ fibrillation, disaggregating Aβ aggregation, thereby ensuring neuron high cell viability, which is vital for AD. Meanwhile, the FA-LCDs possess a high-performance capability of oxidative stress alleviation and inflammation reduction. Due to the excellent BBB penetration ability, after treatment with this FA-LCDs-mediated treatment strategy, the Aβ burden, the ROS accumulation, neuron damages, and cognitive deficits of AD mice are significantly alleviated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process of LCDs and FA-LCDs, and the mechanism of FA-LCDs for treatment AD by penetrating the BBB, inhibiting Aβ fibrillation, degrading Aβ plaque and clearing excessive ROS.

(a) TEM and HR-TEM images of LCDs. (b) TEM and HR-TEM images of FA-LCDs. (c) The particle size distribution diagram of LCDs and FA-LCDs. (d) XPS wide-scan spectra of LCDs and FA-LCDs. (e) The atomic percent of LCDs and FA-LCDs. XPS survey spectrum, the high-resolution C 1 s (f and i), high-resolution N 1 s (g and j), and high-resolution O 1 s (h and k) spectrum of LCDs and FA-LCDs.

(a) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 (20 μM) in the absence or presence of inhibitors (Including FA, LCDs and FA-LCDs). (b) Corresponding inhibition efficiency of inhibitors for Aβ42 aggregation process. (c) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 aggregations (20 μM) in the absence or presence of inhibitors. (d) Corresponding disaggregation efficiency of inhibitors for Aβ42 aggregations. (e) TEM images of Aβ42 monomers and Aβ42 aggregations incubated in the absence and presence of inhibitors. (f) and (g) Corresponding size distribution histogram of TEM results (e). (h) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles to quantify the inhibition of Aβ42 (20 μM) in the absence or presence of FA-LCDs at different concentrations. (i) Corresponding inhibition efficiency of FA-LCDs for Aβ42 aggregation process. (j) ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 aggregates in the presence and absence of FA-LCDs at different concentrations. Data are presented as the mean ± SEM, n = 3 (****, p < 0.0001).

(a) The CLSM images of control, 6E10, Aβ42 (20 μM), and Aβ42 treated with FA-LCDs group. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (Blue), Aβ42 were stained with 6E10 (Green), and the red FL signal from LCDs. (b) The CLSM image and corresponding Pearson’s correlation coefficient, and (c) the corresponding line series analysis of the CLSM image. (d) Tyrosine fluorescence changes of Aβ42 in the presence of different concentrations of FA-LCDs. (e) The CLSM images of HT22 cells treated with FITC-Aβ42 (20 μM) and FITC-Aβ42/inhibitors (Including FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs), and (f) the corresponding quantitative analysis of FITC fluorescence. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue). The green fluorescence was coming from FITC-Aβ42. (g) The different concentrations of Aβ42 mediated cytotoxicity of HT22 cells. (h) Aβ42 and Aβ42/FA-LCDs mediated cytotoxicity of HT22 cells. (i) Aβ42 and Aβ42/inhibitors mediated cytotoxicity of HT22 cells. Data are presented as the mean ± SEM, n = 3 (*p < 0.05; **< 0.01; **** p < 0.0001). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ROS and neuroinflammation studies. (a) The CLSM images of HT22 cells treated with Aβ/FA-LCDs and (b) the corresponding quantitative analysis of ROS related green fluorescence. (c) The CLSM images of HT22 cells treated with Aβ/inhibitors (Including FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs) and (d) the corresponding quantitative analysis of ROS related green fluorescence. The ROS-positive cells show green fluorescence. The nuclei were stained with Hoechst (blue fluorescence). (e) The ROS (DCF fluorescence signal) quantitative analysis of HT22 cells treated with Aβ/FA-LCDs by flow cytometry. (f) The elimination efficiency and absorbance intensity change at 517 nm of DPPH after different concentrations of FA-LCDs treatment. LPS-induced neuroinflammation model: ELISA detection results of IL-6 (g) and TNF-α (h). Data are presented as the mean ± SEM, n = 6 (*p < 0.05; **< 0.01; *** p < 0.001; **** p < 0.0001). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

BBB permeability studies. (a) The CLSM images of bEnd.3 cells treated with FA-LCDs (10 μg mL−1) from 0 h to 24 h and (b) the quantitative analysis of intracellular uptake by flow cytometry. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue fluorescence). Cytomembrane was stained with Dio (green fluorescence). (c) In vitro BBB model established using bEnd.3 cells. (d) The CLSM images of uptake inhibitors (Including FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs) by HT22 cells in basal chamber after incubation with inhibitors for 24 h and (e) the quantitative analysis of intracellular uptake by flow cytometry. (f) Transport ratio of inhibitors in BBB model. (g) The distribution of inhibitors in the hippocampus of the mouse brain and (h) the quantitative analysis of inhibitors fluorescence intensity in the hippocampus of the mouse brain. Data are presented as the mean ± SEM, n = 3 (**< 0.01). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ideal therapeutic strategies for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) should be inhibiting β-amyloid (Aβ) aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) on the premise that drugs can cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB). However, efficient AD therapy is unrealizable due to the lack of multi-target therapy function and poor BBB penetration. Herein, multi-target nanosweepers (FA-LCDs) prepared with ferulic acid (FA) and L-glutamate-derived carbon dots (LCDs) are developed to penetrate the BBB, inhibit Aβ fibrillation, degrade Aβ plaque and clear excessive ROS. Interestingly, although FA has excellent ROS clearance ability, it promotes the aggregation of amyloid, which has potential hidden dangers for treating AD. Rationally designing, modifying FA on the surface of LCDs endows FA-LCDs superior therapeutic performance compared with FA or LCDs alone, and even realizes the superposition of performance. Consequently, the FA-LCDs can target Aβ binding for effectively inhibiting Aβ fibrillation, disaggregating Aβ aggregation, thereby ensuring neuron high cell viability, which is vital for AD. Meanwhile, the FA-LCDs possess a high-performance capability of oxidative stress alleviation and inflammation reduction. Due to the excellent BBB penetration ability, after treatment with this FA-LCDs-mediated treatment strategy, the Aβ burden, the ROS accumulation, neuron damages, and cognitive deficits of AD mice are significantly alleviated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the unremitting and irreversible neurodegenerative disorders among the elderly characterized by progressive cognitive dysfunction and memory loss, Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is turning into a global burden. More than 55 million people suffering from AD around the world and expected to exceed 139 million people worldwide by 2050, which has been considered as one of the most significant healthcare challenges in the 21st century [1–3]. AD’s main pathogenic factors have long been connected with the abnormal aggregation of amyloid-β peptide (Aβ) in the brain, which is found to trigger reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inflammation, and initiate the onset of synaptic and neuronal dysfunction and loss [4,5]. Therefore, numerous therapies focusing on inhibiting Aβ aggregation or eliminating excessive Aβ aggregates, have been developed to improve AD [6–9]. However, the challenges of limited potency, the intricate pathogenesis of AD, and the existence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB), make drug for AD treatment development extremely difficult. Therefore, efficient therapeutic strategies concurrently targeting both Aβ and other biomarkers while breaking through the BBB emerge as a powerful tool to combat the complex pathogenesis of AD.

As a matter of fact, an imbalance between the oxidation and antioxidant defense, especially the generation of excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) is now emerging as a critical detrimental factor driving AD pathogenesis [10,11]. Overproduction of ROS ultimately leads to elevated oxidative stress in brain regions, which damages cellular macromolecules and induces apoptosis and necrosis of neurons [12–14]. A great deal of evidence unequivocally suggests that the markers of oxidative stress (especially ROS) increase in the brains of patients with AD and also in the experimental animal models of AD [10,11,15–19]. Even the accumulation of the classical markers of Aβ along with the mitochondrial dysfunction escalates ROS in the brain [4,7,20,21]. The high level of ROS promotes structural and functional disruption of the neurons, promote Aβ aggregation, ultimately lead to the loss of synapses [13,22–25]. The preclinical evidence and animal model experiments clearly indicated an increase in the oxidative stress in the hippocampus and cortex of the brain during AD progression [26–28]. Moreover, a direct link between Aβ aggregation and ROS, the related research indicated that ROS may cause Aβ aggregation by free radical polymerization and directly oxidizes amyloid, altering its structure and function to make it more susceptible to aggregation [29]. In addition, ROS also can be produced in the presence of Aβ and metal ion. Aβ and metal ions form metal-Aβ complex species and redox metal ions such as Cu lead to the production of ROS. [30,31]. In addition, the synergistic interplay between Aβ and ROS manifests through a self-amplifying cycle of oxidative damage. Pathological Aβ accumulation and increased amyloid precursor protein (APP) expression initiate an oxidative stress surge that exacerbates ROS-mediated neurotoxicity. Crucially, ROS-induced Aβ aggregation acts as a central pathogenic driver in Alzheimer's disease, disrupting cellular redox homeostasis through multiple mechanisms. This oxidative imbalance induces synaptic dysfunction and potentiates neuronal vulnerability through lipid peroxidation and protein oxidation cascades. Notably, this pathological relationship exhibits bidirectional reinforcement. Elevated ROS levels reciprocally enhance Aβ production and promote its oligomerization, while age-related mitochondrial dysfunction creates a pernicious microenvironment for APP processing. As mitochondrial efficiency declines in the aging brain, impaired APP metabolism leads to preferential amyloidogenic processing, resulting in accelerated Aβ plaque formation. This vicious cycle ultimately overwhelms neuronal antioxidant defenses, establishing the characteristic neuropathological [32]. All the above evidence indicates that ROS and Aβ aggregation should be regarded as an important target for both AD treatments. Therefore, antioxidants and anti-amyloid aggregation inhibitors are being increasingly investigated through preclinical and clinical trials for their efficacy as anti-AD agents and cognitive boosters. Among the development of therapeutic agents against AD, natural molecules have received extensive attention from researchers. Some natural molecules, such as (−)-epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG), curcumin (CUR), ferulic acid (FA), salidroside (Sal), resveratrol (Res), and quercitrin have been reported to have biological effects of inhibiting β- and γ-secretase, inhibiting Aβ aggregation, inhibiting tau hyperphosphorylation and intracellular neuro-fibrillary tangles, removing ROS and neuroinflammation [33–39]. For example, Kim et al. found that EGCG could protect the BV2 microglia cells from oxidative and/or nitrosative cell death caused by Aβ aggregation [36]. They further clarified the intracellular signaling events leading to neuroprotection against Aβ-stimulated oxidative and/or nitrosative cell death in BV2 cells. They examined the EGCG-augmented antioxidant self-defense capacity via regulating intracellular glutathione metabolism. By examining banked cerebrospinal fluid and plasma samples from a subset of AD subjects, Moussa et al. found that resveratrol decreased cerebrospinal fluid metalloproteinases-9, reduced Aβ42 levels, and modulated neuro-inflammation [39]. Among them, as a potential pharmacophore, FA possesses structural motifs that contribute to this compound’s free radical scavenging and amyloid aggregation inhibiting capability [40–43]. Therefore, FA has been widely used as an antioxidant for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, inhibition of Aβ aggregation, and protects the neuronal cells from oxidative damage. In vitro and in vivo studies, it has been shown that FA could disaggregate Aβ fibrils, inhibit Aβ aggregation, protect against lipid peroxidation, protect the brain from Aβ toxicity and decrease Aβ deposition in the AD mice model [40,42]. However, related research work indicated that FA has poor inhibition efficiency and bioavailability of Aβ in vivo. Furthermore, relatively low lipophilicity implicates poor blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability and poor aqueous solubility, which are considered major limitations associated with FA a druggable agent for AD. Therefore, developing a strategy with enhanced therapeutic efficiency of natural medicine molecules is necessary and remains a significant challenge as a drug candidate for treatment AD.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial, are gaining increasing attention in the biomaterial field due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, high water dispersibility, low price of production [44]. Moreover, CDs have been applied to research the treatment of AD due to the ability of BBB penetration, inhibition of amyloid, scavenging ROS, and resisting inflammation [45–52]. For example, Lin et al. synthesized EGCG-derived CDs (E-CPDs), successfully used to inhibit Aβ fibrillization and scavenge intracellular ROS [53]. Bao et al. proposed chiral CDs using D-cysteine as the chiral carbon source, and found that D-Cys-CDs inhibited Cu2+ induced Aβ aggregation, scavenged intracellular ROS, inflammation and cytotoxicity inducing by Aβ42-Cu2+ [54]. Based on the previous research [49–51,55], our group found that CDs could efficiently inhibit Aβ aggregation, disaggregate amyloid aggregation by photocatalytic methods. In addition, CDs could across the BBB, scavenge ROS and resist neuroinflammation [51]. More strikingly, as excellent drug carriers, CDs were selected to combine various natural molecules for improving the BBB penetration and therapeutic efficiency of the drug [46,49]. Collectively, the ideal CDs supported multifunctional nanosystem is deemed an intriguing candidate to realize the multitarget therapy of AD by effectively penetrating the BBB, suppressing Aβ aggregation, promoting Aβ plaque clearance, and eliminating ROS in the AD microenvironment.

In this work, we report BBB-penetrating, Aβ-targeting, amyloid-degrading and ROS-regulating FA-LCDs via selecting appropriate CDs and co-assembly strategy of CDs and natural molecules to resolve the above long-standing challenges in AD. Firstly, we synthesize a kind of L-glutamate-derived CDs (LCDs) with red emission through hydrothermal reaction. LCDs possess outstanding ability for BBB penetration, Aβ aggregation inhibition, ROS regulation. Then, the FA-LCDs with excellent performance are fabricated by modifying antioxidant molecule FA on the surface of LCDs. As shown in scheme 1 , the resulting FA-LCDs generate new advantages neither existing in FA nor LCDs: (i) FA promotes Aβ aggregation and assists Aβ fibrillar stability. The introduction of LCDs significantly improved Aβ inhibition and amyloid fibrils disaggregation efficiency of FA-LCDs. (ii) By introducing FA into the FA-LCDs, ROS clearance and anti-inflammatory properties of FA-LCDs were significantly improved. (iii) The co-assembly of FA and LCDs significantly improves the BBB-transport ratio of FA-LCDs compared with FA and LCDs. In addition, the FA-LCDs specifically inhibit the Aβ fibrils formation and disassemble the Aβ plaques via strong affinity interactions for Aβ species. Moreover, the FA-LCDs strongly inhibit the potent harmful reaggregation process of the degraded Aβ fragments, remodel the cerebral redox balance, and reverse neurotoxicity to acquire remarkable behavioral and cognitive improvements in the AD mice model. Overall, the achievement of the FA-LCDs treatment strategy, as a proof-of-concept, has well settled the dilemma of the Aβ fibril and inflammation in AD, thus might inspire more advanced visual nanotheranostic for other neurodegenerative diseases to guard our brain health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, preparation, and characterization of the FA-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above guidelines, we synthesized LCDs and FA-LCDs according to the route as shown in Scheme 1. The representative morphology was verified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM results showed uniform spherical structures of LCDs and FA-LCDs with average sizes around 3 and 4 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a, b and c). Moreover, there was no significant aggregation in particle size after incubating LCDs and FA-LCDs in PBS, indicating the excellent stability of the FA-LCDs. Meanwhile, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) results suggest that the lattice fringes of LCDs and FA-LCDs are identical to an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1a, b), which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [56,57]. The main reason why nanomaterials can be applied in biological field is their distinctively structural characteristics. To delve into the elemental composition and structure of LCDs and FA-LCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was exhibited. As shown in Fig. 1d, LCDs and FA-LCDs exhibit three peaks at 284.2, 401.5, and 531.1 eV, which belong to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively [53]. As shown in Fig. 1d, three identical significant peaks but with varied intensity were found in the full scan spectra, i.e. C (284.2 eV, 73.1 %), N (401.5 eV, 8.95 %) and O (531.1 eV, 17.5 %) for LCDs, while C (283.8 eV, 69.79 %), N (399.5 eV, 7.57 %) and O (530.2 eV, 22.63 %) for FA-LCDs. Compared with LCDs, FA-LCDs had reduced C content of 3.31 %, N content of 1.38 % and an elevated O content up to 5.13 % (Fig. 1e). The decrease of C and N content and the increase of O content in FA-LCD are mainly due to the modification of FA that changes the composition ratio of element content. The high resolution C1s spectrum of LCDs and FA-LCDs indicates the existence of four types of characteristic peaks, including C=C/C-C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (285.7 eV), C=O (286.5 eV), and −O-C=O (288.3 eV) (Fig. 1f and i) [57]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of LCDs and FA-LCDs displays two characteristic peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.2 eV (Fig. 1g and j), representing N-H and C-N-C, respectively [58]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of LCDs and FA-LCDs displays two characteristic peaks at 532.6 eV and 535.4 eV (Fig. 1h and k), representing C-O-C/C-O-H and C=O, respectively [58]. Obviously, FA-CDs contain more C-O-C/C-O-H content than LCDs, indicating FA have been modified to FA-LCDs surface and cause a change in the chemical structure of LCDs. From the above analysis, it can be seen that FA-LCD is different from LCDs in morphology with comparable size, chemical structure and element composition. The surface functional groups of LCDs and FA-LCDs were characterized by FTIR spectrum in Supporting Information, Fig. S1. The characteristic peaks at 2926 and 2850 cm−1 are due to the benzene ring’s stretching vibrational absorption of –OH [53,54]. The peak at 1623 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of −C=O in the carboxyl group. The peak at 1582, 1519, and 1470 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of the C=C double bond in aromatics [53,54]. The characteristic peak at 1316 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of C-N. Compared with LCDs, FA-LCDs exhibit a distinct characteristic peak at 1217 cm−1 [50,53], belonging to the stretching vibrations of C-O in the phenol structure, probably due to the phenol structure introduced after FA modification. Based on the above analysis, LCDs and FA-LCDs almost shared the same surface groups, such as –COOH, –OH, –NH2, and –CO-NH-, which may lay the foundation for their multiple functions. However, FA-LCDs have a significantly higher C-O-C/C-O-H content compared to LCDs in XPS results, and exhibit a distinct characteristic peak at 1217 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum, which is mainly attributed to the modification of FA on LCDs. Following, the effective loading efficiency of FA-LCDs for FA were calculated with 35.8 % (Supporting Information, Fig. S2).

Due to their excellent fluorescent properties, CDs are widely used in the biomedical field as fluorescent labels. Therefore, the fluorescent properties of LCDs and FA-LCDs were investigated by a 3D excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S3a, a maximum emission at 660 nm upon exciting at 550 nm was observed for LCDs. After modification of FA, the excitation-emission fluorescent matrix (Supporting Information, Fig. S3b) of FA-LCDs showed excitation-dependent emission, and the maximum excitation wavelengths of 479 nm and 650 nm, respectively. The red fluorescence of FA-LCDs indicated their potential as fluorescent probes. In addition, when excited at the optimal wavelength, the absolute fluorescence quantum yields for LCDs and FA-LCDs is about 18.9 and 13.8 %, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition amyloidogenesis and disaggregation amyloid fibrils by FA-LCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Previous report has indicated that the strong binding between inhibitors and amyloid is mainly due to their amphiphilic properties, which facilitate multiple types of interactions, which include hydrogen bonding between the polar residues of the amyloid monomers and the epoxide, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the inhibitors, aromatic stacking, and electrostatic attraction between the positively charged residues of the amyloid monomers and the carboxylic acid groups on the inhibitors [59]. Therefore, the internal structure and surface groups inspired us to investigate whether FA-LCDs had a solid inhibitory effect on amyloidogenesis. Due to the well-known neurotoxicity, Aβ42 is the ideal amyloid model. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the aggregation kinetics of Aβ42 showed that the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 alone did not change significantly from 0 to 26 h, and the ThT fluorescence intensity increased rapidly after 26 h, reaching a plateau after 32 h of incubation, indicating the formation of Aβ aggregates. Incubating Aβ42 with FA and comparing with Aβ42 alone, the aggregation kinetics of Aβ42 showed that the initial stabilization phase was shortened (22 h), forming a long aggregation time, and the plateau was significantly prolonged (40 h). As a result, the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 together with FA more than that of Aβ42 alone at the plateau. Related results also can be seen intuitively from Fig. 2b, it is indicated that FA not only fails to inhibit Aβ42 aggregation, but even has the property of assisting Aβ42 aggregation to some extent. With the addition of LCDs or FA-LCDs, the ThT fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced, and the inhibition efficiency of FA-LCDs reached to 63 %. Subsequently, the disaggregation capacity of FA-LCDs was also investigated. The Aβ42 monomers were incubated for 24 h and the ThT kinetics assay of Aβ42 fibrillation was performed. At 24 h, inhibitors were added to the above samples while being continually incubated. As shown in Fig. 2c, the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 fibrils continually increased after adding FA, indicating that FA can’t disaggregate Aβ42 fibrils. Contrarily, the ThT fluorescence intensity significantly decreased after adding LCDs or FA-LCDs, and the disaggregation efficiency is all over 50 % (Fig. 2d). To further confirm the inhibitory effect of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs, the sample at the end of each ThT experiment was collected and analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2e, the Aβ42 alone aggregated into typical long and branched fibrils, and the mean length is about 572 nm. After adding FA, the formed Aβ42 fibrils get shorter, but the Aβ42 amyloid plaques become more clumped compared with Aβ42 alone, as shown in the TEM images. As expected, LCDs and FA-LCDs effectively inhibited the transformation of Aβ42 monomer into fibril and amyloid plaques, the corresponding statistical lengths were 74 and 53 nm (Fig. 2f), respectively. In addition, the disaggregation effect of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs were also evaluated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2e, the Aβ42 fibrils alone maintain densely tangled fibrils. However, the Aβ42 fibrils treated with FA become more clumped and form huge amyloid plaques. As expected, LCDs and FA-LCDs effectively degraded Aβ42 fibrils into amorphous fragments, the corresponding statistical length of the were 103 and 82 nm (Fig. 2g), respectively. These results indicated that FA can induce Aβ42 fibrillation and cause Aβ42 fibrils into huge aggregates, which will raising the risk of amyloid deposition and even apoptosis of neurons in AD brain. Thankfully, after leading LCDs into inhibitors (FA-LCDs), the defect of FA was effectively avoided, and FA-LCDs exhibited excellent ability at inhibiting Aβ42 fibrillation and disaggregating amyloid aggregates. Furthermore, the concentration-dependent inhibition and disaggregation abilities of FA-LCDs were demonstrated. As exhibited in Fig. 2h, i, j, and k, FA-LCDs inhibited Aβ42 aggregation and disaggregated Aβ42 aggregates in a concentration-dependent manner. Notably, FA-LCDs could completely inhibit the aggregation of Aβ42 at 100 μg mL−1, and inhibit about 90.0 % of ThT fluorescence intensity at 5 μg mL−1. Meanwhile, FA-LCDs also could completely disaggregate Aβ42 aggregates at 100 μg mL−1, and disaggregate about 94.6 % of ThT fluorescence intensity at 5 μg mL−1. The feasibility of FA-LCDs at inhibition and disaggregation was also investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging in Supporting Information, Fig. S5, Aβ42 alone aggregated into densely tangled fibrils, similar to previous reports [48]. FA-LCDs inhibited Aβ42 aggregation and dissociated fibrils into minimum pieces. As the concentration of FA-LCDs increased, the mean length and height of the Aβ42 aggregation decreased. The inhibition efficiency of FA-LCDs was also estimated using CD spectroscopy (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). After incubation for 24 h, Aβ42 samples were detected with a negative band at 218 nm, which indicated the conversion from a random structure to β-sheet. For the FA-LCDs-treated group, the negative band of Aβ42 at 218 nm reduced, indicating that the β-sheet structure of Aβ42 was partially inhibited. Moreover, the corresponding β-sheet component of Aβ42 decreased from 28.4 to 17.1 %. The β-sheet structure plays a key role in the aggregation of Aβ42 through hydrophobic interactions. Collectively, the interaction between FA-LCDs and Aβ42 is driven chiefly by hydrophobic interactions, accompanied by structural changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted binding affinity between Aβ42 and FA-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: FA-LCDs must attach specifically to Aβ42 to effectively suppress Aβ42 aggregation and clean amyloid plaques. Based on the previous analysis, FA-LCDs have exhibited excellent inhibition and disaggregation ability. To verify the targeted binding affinity between Aβ42 and FA-LCDs, we used immunofluorescence colocalization experiments. HT22 cells were used as neuron models and treated with Aβ42 and inhibitors. After incubation, as Aβ specific antibodies, 6E10 were added into the HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 and inhibitors [60]. Because of the recognizing ability for amino acid residue 1–16 of Aβ, 6E10 can bind to Aβ42 monomer, parenchymal plaques. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of HT22 cells revealed that 6E10-positive Aβ42 species were substantially colocalized with LCDs or FA-LCDs compared with control and negative groups. The Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 6E10-positive Aβ42 species/LCDs and Aβ42 species/FA-LCDs are 0.9 and 0.79 (Fig. 3b), respectively. In addition, the corresponding line series analysis further verified their strong colocalization (Fig. 3c). To further investigate the binding affinity between FA-LCDs and Aβ42, tyrosine (Tyr) fluorescence spectra in Aβ42 were measured after treatment with different concentrations of FA-LCDs. Tyr is located at the 10th site in the sequence of Aβ42 which has self-fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm [61]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Tyr fluorescence intensity decreased obviously after adding of FA-LCDs compared with Aβ42 alone. With the increase of the concentration (5, 10, 25, 50, 100 μg mL−1) of FA-LCDs, the fluorescence intensity of Tyr decreased by about 15 %, 22 %, 59 %, 89 % and 97 %, respectively. Besides, the fluorescence peak had a red shift from 304 nm to 315 nm, indicating that FA-LCDs may interact with Aβ42, which can quench the fluorescence of Tyr.

Previous study indicates that Aβ42-mediated neurotoxicity from the presence of Aβ42 aggregates, which can target the plasma membrane, causing increased membrane permeability and metabolic problems in cells [6,8]. Due to the excellent ability of targeted binding affinity and inhibition efficiency for Aβ42 aggregation, we speculate that FA-LCDs could effectively suppress Aβ42 aggregation mediated cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 3e, compared with the control group, HT22 cells co-incubated with FITC labeled Aβ42 (FITC-Aβ42) for 24 h, large amounts of Aβ42 species (green color) are clustered around HT22 cells, implying that Aβ42 species were firmly bound to cell membranes. As expected, the Aβ42 species-associated fluorescence intensity markedly decreased in the group of LCDs incubated with Aβ42 and HT22 cells. Furthermore, the vast majority of Aβ42 species have been cleared after adding FA-LCDs, indicating FA-LCDs can mitigate the adhesion of Aβ42 species to the cell surface and reduce the interaction of Aβ42 with the cell membrane. The corresponding mean fluorescence intensity analyzed from confocal images also confirmed related conclusions (Fig. 3f).

To assess inhibitor detoxification, CCK-8 assays were carried out using HT22 cell lines. Fig. 3g shows that within the studied dose range (5–100 μg mL−1), FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs showed no apparent cytotoxicity, preserving over 95 % cell viability. However, introducing Aβ42 lowered the vitality of HT22 cells, with the lowest cell activity achieved at 20 μ M, which indicates that Aβ42 can induce HT22 cell death. Fortunately, FA-LCDs dose-dependently attenuated the toxic effect of Aβ42, increasing the cell viability to more than 95.6 % at 100 µg mL−1 (Fig. 3h). Meanwhile, FA-LCDs showed a more substantial inhibitory impact than FA and LCDs, showing that the combination of FA and LCDs significantly increased the inhibitor's inhibitory effect on amyloid toxicity (Fig. 3i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS elimination and inflammation alleviation by inhibitors in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: High levels of ROS and Aβ aggregates in the AD microenvironment might activate microglia, causing them to release proinflammatory cytokines and generate more ROS, worsening the symptoms [8,14,15]. Therefore, intracellular ROS scavenging activity of inhibitors was evaluated with HT22 cells by 2′-7′dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA assay). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, compared to the Aβ species group, the FA-LCDs group revealed inhibited ROS level with weak green fluorescence from DCF, and the green fluorescence becomes weaker and weaker with FA-LCDs concentration (Fig. 4a and b). After quantitative analysis by flow cytometry, the addition of 5 and 10 μg mL−1 FA-LCDs treatment, the level of ROS was reduced from 82.1 % to 73.1 % and 59 %, respectively (Fig. 4e). However, the addition of FA and LCDs reduced green fluorescence, FA-LCDs performed best in ROS removal in cells (Fig. 4c). To explore the in vivo therapy function of FA-LCDs, we analyzed in situ ROS change in the ex vivo brain after intraperitoneal injection of FA-LCDs one time, the results revealed an efficient decrease of excess ROS induced (Supporting Information, Fig. S7a and b). Then, reduced fluorescence signals and intensity of ROS level in the brain slices stained by DCFH-DA verified the FA-LCDs could efficiently decrease the oxidation level in the brain (Supporting Information, Fig. S7c and d). These findings suggested that the combination of FA and LCDs endows FA-LCDs a strong antioxidant potential. DPPH was used as the model radical to research the anti-oxidative mechanism of the produced FA-LCDs. As an N-centered free radical, DPPH had a distinct absorption peak at 515 nm. When DPPH encountered free radical scavengers, the peak decreased because of the development of the DPPH complex [62]. As shown in Fig. 4f and Supporting Information, Fig. S8, the peak intensity of DPPH at 515 nm decreased as the concentration grew from 0 to 100 μg mL−1. The scavenging efficacy of LCQDs for DPPH free radicals increased in proportion to their concentration. FA-LCDs demonstrated 60 % efficiency at 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4f and Supporting Information, Fig. S9). As expected, FA-LCDs significantly increased cell viability and restored DCF fluorescence to levels comparable to the control group, while also efficiently reducing the total quantity of DPPF free radicals, confirming their efficacy against ROS in HT22 cells (Fig. 3h and Fig. 4a). Hydroxyl radical and 2, 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) are both ROS-related oxygen radicals. Interestingly, FA-LCDs also can remove these ROS-related free radicals, the corresponding elimination reach to 52 % (for hydroxyl radical) and 26 % (for ABTS) (Supporting Information, Fig. S10), respectively. In addition, the expression levels of neuroinflammatory factors in BV2 cells were also examined. After 24 h of LPS co-incubation, the expression of various proinflammatory cytokines, including IL-6 and TNF-α in BV2 cells were primarily upregulated. Markedly, FA-LCDs could suppress the upregulation of these proinflammatory cytokines (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). Meanwhile, we have also assessed the anti-inflammatory properties of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs, FA-LCDs led to the significant elimination of TNF-α and IL-6 (Fig. 4g and h), indicating an efficient alleviation of inflammation by the ROS-scavenging FA-LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular uptake and BBB permeability by inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely acknowledged that AD inhibitors have a minimal therapeutic effect due to the presence of BBB. Cerebral microvascular endothelial cells play an essential role in the composition of BBB [63]. Therefore, the mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3 cells) were selected to investigate the intracellular uptake of inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after 24 h of co-incubation with bEnd.3 cells, the fluorescence signal of FA-LCDs was substantially higher than that of the control group, indicating that FA-LCDs were endocytosed. In addition, the intracellular uptake of FA-LCDs in bEnd.3 cells were also measured using flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. 5b, bEnd.3 cells in the FA-LCDs-treated group from 0.5 to 24 h, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the incubation time, and the intracellular uptake rate reached 82.8 % after 24 h, which suggested that FA-LCDs had a more extraordinary ability to penetrate the BBB. Based on the above results, we further used the transwells model to investigate the BBB permeability of FA-LCDs (Fig. 5c). After adding FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs in apical chamber and incubating for 24 h, the stronger fluorescence signals (red color) of LCDs, and FA-LCDs group in bottom chamber were detected, revealing that LCDs and FA-LCDs efficiently permeated the BBB (Fig. 5d). The intracellular uptake of HT22 in bottom chamber were measured by flow cytometry, the intracellular uptake about LCDs and FA-LCDs reached 99.6 and 81.9 % (Fig. 5e), respectively. For evaluating the BBB penetration efficiency in transwell model, the transport ratios of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs were calculated to be 32.3 %, 45.6 %, and 64 % (Fig. 5f), respectively. These findings strongly suggested that FA-LCDs had the potential to deliver therapeutic drugs to the brain. Subsequently, BBB penetrability was tested by injecting inhibitors into mice’s tail veins. The obtained brain tissue was investigated through an Ex vivo fluorescence imaging assay. As shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S12, mouse brain tissues showed a distinct fluorescent signal compared to controls after 12 h of treatment with LCDs or FA-LCDs. Moreover, the mouse brain tissues obtained from above were collected and sliced. The obtained tissue slices were detected using CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5g and h, stronger fluorescence signals were detected in the hippocampus of LCDs and FA-LCDs group compared with the control group. In order to assess the amounts of FA-LCDs in brain during 24 h, we obtained the fluorescence signal of FA-LCDs from mice brain using in vitro-tissue imaging after intraperitoneal injection (inject time: 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 6, 12 and 24 h). The related results indicated that FA-LCDs began into the brain at 0.5 h after injection, and the amounts of FA-LCDs entering the brain increased with time increase, reaching the maximum at 24 h (Supporting Information, Fig. S13). These finding strongly suggested that FA-LCDs had the potential to deliver therapeutic drugs to the brain and could be chosen for further relative biological assays to evaluate the therapeutic potential in combating AD.

In practical clinical applications, inhibitors come into direct contact with tissues and blood. Hence, their biocompatibility needs to be assessed. Before proceeding the in vivo experiment, the applicable working concentrations of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs were determined through hemolysis assay [64]. As shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S14, the hemolysis assay results of FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs shows almost no toxicity up to a concentration of 100 μg mL−1 for both substances, which representing a high bioavailability. Meanwhile, the standard CCK-8 method was used to determine the effect of inhibitors in each group on the viability of HT22 cells. As shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S15, for HT22 cells, when the concentration of inhibitors (including FA, LCDs, and FA-LCDs) was 100 μg mL−1, the survival rate of inhibitors was still more than 88 %. Moreover, the survival rate of FA-LCDs was still more than 90 % at 200 μg mL−1. It was proved that the modifying FA on the surface of LCDs effectively reduced the cytotoxicity of FA-LCDs. After 12 h treatment with LCDs, and FA-LCDs, mice were sacrificed, their major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and brain) were collected for further analysis. As visualized via fluorescence imaging in vivo systems (Supporting Information, Fig. S16), the fluorescence imaging significantly demonstrated that FA-LCDs accumulated in the brain and liver tissue after 12 h intraperitoneal injection, confirming that FA-LCDs were efficiently delivered into the brain and cleared mainly by liver in comparison with the other organs (Supporting Information, Fig. S16). Subsequently, the organs listed above were then collected and sliced for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. As demonstrated in Supporting Information, Fig. S17, no noticeable pathological abnormalities were found. Moreover, the results of the blood biochemistry analysis showed that FA-LCDs did not reduce liver (alanine transaminase [ALT], indicators of hepatic toxicity; Supporting Information, Fig. S18a) or kidney (creatinine [CRE], indicators of renal toxicity; Supporting Information, Fig. S18b) function. There results both indicating that FA-LCDs had a high biocompatibility and might be employed for further AD treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo synergistic therapy effects on AD mice
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the preceding studies, we hypothesized that FA-LCDs may have a therapeutic function in AD. Subsequently, we studied the therapeutic activity of FA-LCDs in AD mice using the approach (Fig. 6 a), which includes the i.v. injection of PBS or FA-LCDs once a day and performed 21 times of injections. Cognitive impairment is one of the typical features of AD. After the APP/PS1 mice were treated with PBS (AD), FA (AD:FA), LCDs (AD:LCDs) and FA-LCDs (AD:FA-LCDs) respectively, for 21 days, their spatial learning and memory behavior was examined by the Morris water maze (MWM) experiment. After the initial 5-day training period, the escape latency in AD group was shorter than that of WT group, and indicated that the mice in the AD group exhibited an impaired learning ability compared with the WT mice (Fig. 6b). Treating the APP/PS1 mice with FA and LCDs showed no significant improvement on their learning behavior. However, after the FA-LCDs treatment, the escape latency of the APP/PS1 mice was reduced to a level comparable to the WT mice, indicating an exceptional therapeutic effect of FA-LCDs (Fig. 6c). The ability of FA-LCDs to rescue the learning and memory behavior was further confirmed by their probe trials after the platform was removed. The mice in the FA-LCDs treatment group spent a longer time in the targeted platform quadrant and crossed over the hidden platform more times than those in both AD, AD:FA, AD:LCDs groups (Fig. 6d and e). These results together demonstrated the FA-LCDs treatment could improve the activity of spatial learning memory and recognition capability of AD mice.

We also explored FA-LCDs’ ability to clear Aβ burdens and inhibit neuronal loss in the APP/PS1 mice through immunohistochemical analysis, immunofluorescence analysis and Nissl staining analysis. The immunohistochemical analysis proved the decreased amount of Aβ species in the FA-LCDs treatment group compared AD, AD:FA, and AD:LCDs group (Fig. 6f and Supporting Information, Fig. S19). In addition, immunofluorescence imaging was used to detect the quantity of Aβ42 species in hippocampus and cortex. As shown in Fig. 6g and Supporting Information, Fig. S20, large amounts of fluorescence signals from Aβ42 species in APP/PS1 mice treated with PBS were detected in hippocampus and cortex. As expected, the APP/PS1 mice treated with FA-LCDs exhibited significantly weaker Aβ42 species fluorescence than those treated with FA or LCDs. This demonstrates the efficient Aβ42 species clearance. Finally, Nissl staining analysis (Supporting Information, Fig. S21) results reveal that the AD group, FA or LCDs treated APP/PS1 mice had few Nissl bodies compared to FA-LCDs treated group. Collectively, the above results unambiguously demonstrated that FA-LCDs could significantly attenuate Aβ burdens and protect neurons, so that the learning and memory functions of the APP/PS1 transgenic mice could be restored through FA-LCDs-enabled multi-target therapy.

In summary, we reported multi-target nanosweepers prepared with FA and LCDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation and regulating ROS levels, thereby enabling therapeutic studies of AD. The prepared nanosweepers holds multiple functions while avoiding the side effects of the traditional molecule ferulic acid-promoting amyloid aggregation. Introducing fluorescence carbon dots significantly improved Aβ inhibition and amyloid fibrils disaggregation efficiency of FA-LCDs. By introducing FA into the FA-LCDs, ROS clearance and anti-inflammatory properties of FA-LCDs were significantly improved. In addition, the FA-LCDs can specifically inhibit the Aβ fibrils formation and disassemble the Aβ plaques via strong affinity interactions for Aβ species. Moreover, the combination of FA and LCDs significantly improves the BBB-transport ratio of FA-LCDs, thereby effectively inhibiting Aβ aggregation, promoting Aβ phagocytosis, and decreasing the level of ROS in vitro and in vivo. As a result, the above therapy effect of FA-LCDs together reverses the neurotoxicity and achieves effective behavioral and cognitive improvements in the AD mice model. This research is generalizable to the fabrication of nanodrugs against related neurodegenerative diseases. Our goal is to provide insight into the development of advanced anti-neurodegenerative nanodrugs to guard brain health.
